subject: Determinant formulas and cofactors
concepts: The Big Formula for the Determinant: The determinant of an n×n matrix is a sum of n! terms. Each term is a product of exactly n entries, one chosen from each row and each column (i.e., one entry per row and per column, forming a permutation). Each term carries a + or − sign depending on whether the corresponding column permutation is even (even number of swaps to sort it) or odd. In plain terms, to compute a determinant you pick one entry from every row and column in every possible way, multiply them together, attach a sign based on how 'shuffled' your choices are, and add everything up.

Permutations and Signs: Each selection of one entry per row and column corresponds to a permutation of column indices (α, β, γ, …). The sign of a term is +1 if the permutation is even (requires an even number of row/column swaps to restore to identity order) and −1 if odd. For an n×n matrix there are n! permutations, half even and half minus, giving n!/2 positive and n!/2 negative terms.

Cofactors C_ij: The cofactor C_ij of entry a_ij is defined as (−1)^{i+j} times the determinant of the (n−1)×(n−1) submatrix obtained by deleting row i and column j of A. The sign factor (−1)^{i+j} creates a checkerboard pattern of + and − signs across the matrix. Cofactors let you express an n×n determinant in terms of smaller (n−1)×(n−1) determinants, making the computation recursive.

Cofactor Expansion Formula: The determinant of A can be computed by expanding along any row i (or any column): det(A) = a_{i1}C_{i1} + a_{i2}C_{i2} + … + a_{in}C_{in}. This is often called Laplace expansion. In plain language, pick any row (or column), multiply each entry by its cofactor, and sum the results — you always get the determinant regardless of which row or column you choose.

Three Formulas for the Determinant: There are three complementary ways to compute a determinant: (1) the Pivot Formula — det(A) equals the product of the pivots from elimination (fast numerically); (2) the Big Formula — the explicit sum of n! signed products (theoretically complete but impractical for large n); and (3) the Cofactor Formula — a recursive expansion that reduces an n×n determinant to n smaller ones (a useful middle ground). Each encodes the same information in a different form.

Recovering the 2×2 Determinant from First Principles: Using linearity (property 3) applied row by row to [[a,b],[c,d]], the matrix splits into four pieces; two have a column of zeros and vanish, leaving ad (from the identity-like term) and −bc (from the one-swap permutation), giving det = ad − bc. This shows how the three defining properties logically force the familiar formula.

Detecting Singularity via the Big Formula: A matrix is singular (det = 0) if and only if all n! terms in the big formula cancel out (or are zero). One practical check is to look for a linear combination of rows (or columns) that gives zero — this certifies singularity without computing all n! terms.
examples: Deriving det([[a,b],[c,d]]) = ad − bc from the three properties: split row 1 into [a,0] + [0,b], then split row 2, giving four 2×2 matrices; two have a zero column and vanish; the survivors are [[a,0],[0,d]] with det = ad and [[0,b],[c,0]] with det = −bc (one row swap needed), summing to ad − bc.

Writing out all 6 terms (3! = 6) of the 3×3 determinant: the three positive terms correspond to permutations (1,2,3), (2,3,1), (3,1,2) giving a11a22a33, a12a23a31, a13a21a32; the three negative terms correspond to permutations (1,3,2), (2,1,3), (3,2,1) giving −a11a23a32, −a12a21a33, −a13a22a31.

Computing the determinant of a specific 4×4 matrix with many zeros using the big formula: only two of the 24 terms are non-zero — one gives +1 (permutation 4,3,2,1, requiring two swaps) and the other gives −1 (permutation 3,2,1,4, requiring one swap); they cancel to give det = 0, confirming the matrix is singular.
Verifying singularity of the above 4×4 matrix by a row combination: row1 − row2 + row3 − row4 = 0, confirming linear dependence.
Cofactor expansion of the 2×2 matrix [[a,b],[c,d]] along row 1: a·C11 + b·C12 = a·(+d) + b·(−c) = ad − bc.

Cofactor expansion of the 3×3 tri-diagonal matrix A3 = [[1,1,0],[1,1,1],[0,1,1]]: subtract row 3 from row 2 (det unchanged), then expand along the modified row 2 using one nonzero entry; its cofactor is the 2×2 identity-like matrix with det = 1, multiplied by −1 (sign from position), giving det(A3) = −1.

Recursive cofactor formula for the n×n tri-diagonal all-ones matrix: det(A_n) = det(A_{n-1}) − det(A_{n-2}), derived by expanding along row 1 — the leading 1 contributes +det(A_{n-1}), and the adjacent 1 (in position (1,2), sign −1) contributes −det(A_{n-2}) after a further cofactor step.

Computing the sequence of tri-diagonal determinants using the recurrence det(A_n) = det(A_{n-1}) − det(A_{n-2}): det(A1)=1, det(A2)=0, det(A3)=−1, det(A4)=−1, det(A5)=0, det(A6)=1, det(A7)=1, … showing the sequence is periodic with period 6.
Counting n! terms: for a 4×4 matrix there are 4! = 24 terms; for a 5×5 matrix, 5! = 120 terms; illustrating why the big formula is impractical for large n compared to elimination.
analogy: The three formulas for the determinant are compared to three different 'views' of the same object: the pivot formula is like a fast numerical algorithm (an efficient machine), the big formula is like a complete blueprint (shows every component explicitly but is unwieldy), and the cofactor formula is like a recursive instruction manual (breaks a large task into smaller identical tasks).

The checkerboard pattern of + and − signs for cofactors ((−1)^{i+j}) is described as a 'checkerboard' — just like a chessboard alternates black and white squares, the sign of each cofactor alternates with each step in row or column index.

The counter-diagonal (anti-diagonal) of a 4×4 matrix of ones is noted to have a +1 determinant (not −1 as one might expect from the 3×3 case), because placing ones on the anti-diagonal of a 4×4 matrix requires exactly two row swaps (an even number) to reach the identity — illustrating that the 'down-toward-Miami' anti-diagonal mnemonic only works for 3×3.
reviewed_transcript: What if I told you the determinant of any square matrix is secretly just a giant sum of signed products? Here's the idea. Imagine a banquet — one guest per row, one chair per column, no two guests sharing a chair. Every possible seating arrangement contributes exactly one term to the determinant. For a 3×3 matrix, that's 3! = 6 arrangements; for 4×4, it's 24. Each term gets a plus or minus sign depending on how scrambled the seating is from natural order — even permutations get a plus, odd permutations get a minus. Take a 2×2 matrix: ad minus bc. Two arrangements, two signs — that's the whole story. But computing all n! terms gets brutal fast, so we use cofactor expansion instead — a recursive trick that cracks an n×n problem into n smaller ones, like Russian nesting dolls, right down to easy 2×2 base cases. Master this, and you hold the key to solving systems, finding inverses, and unlocking some of the deepest structures in all of mathematics.
